12: ve-achar haesh qol demamah daqah: 'And after the fire the sound of a low whisper' (or 'a still small voice,' KJV). The theophany at Horeb (Elijah's experience in 1 Kgs 19) deliberately echoes Sinai: Elijah is at Horeb (another name for Sinai), the mountain of God; YHWH passes by with wind, earthquake, and fire (the Sinai theophanic elements, Exod 19:16-19). But the climax is not in the dramatic elements but in the qol demamah daqah — the sound of sheer silence, the gentle whisper, the still small voice. YHWH is not absent from the dramatic; but he speaks in the gentle. The NT's Spirit comes both in wind and fire (Acts 2:2-3) and in the interior witness of the heart (Rom 8:16).
4: Elijah’s collapse under the broom tree (ʾetem, Retama raetam, the white-flowering desert shrub that provides shade in the Negev) — wayyišʾal ʾet napšô lāmût, ‘he asked for his soul/life (napšô) to die’ — is the OT’s most direct account of prophetic burnout: the total exhaustion that follows crisis-action, the desire for death at the moment of apparent victory. The napšô (his life, his self, his appetite for existence) is what he asks to die — a request for total cessation. YHWH’s response is not rebuke, not a prophetic commission, not a theological correction — it is provision: the angel touches him twice (wayyigʿō bô) and says qûm ʾᵉḵôl, ‘arise and eat.’ The angel bakes a cake on hot coals and brings a jar of water. Physical sustenance precedes any word of recommission or reorientation (which comes only after Horeb, vv14-18). The pattern of gracious provision before recommissioning appears in John 21:9-12, where the risen Jesus has a charcoal fire (anthrakia) with fish and bread ready for the failed and discouraged disciples before any word of commission is spoken. The qûm ʾᵉḵôl at the broom tree is the OT form of the pastoral principle: the servant is fed and strengthened before he is sent; the shepherd provides for the exhausted before demanding further service.
18: YHWH’s corrective to Elijah’s complaint of total isolation — wᵉhišʾartî bᵉyiśrāʾēl šiḇʿat ʾălāpîm kol habbirkayim ʾăšer lōʾ kārᵉʿû labbāʿal, ‘I have reserved for myself in Israel seven thousand, all the knees that have not bowed to Baal’ — introduces the šᵉʾār (remnant) as a theological category. The verb hišʾartî (hiph’il of šāʾar, ‘I have left/reserved’) is the technical term for the remnant’s existence: YHWH himself has preserved them, not their own resilience or visibility. The number seven thousand is symbolic (seven = completeness; thousands = covenant-fullness), not a census figure. The seven thousand are hidden from Elijah’s perception but known to YHWH — their existence is a matter of divine knowledge and divine preservation, not human observation. Paul in Rom 11:4-5 quotes this verse as the OT proof for the grace-remnant of his own time: ‘what is God’s reply to him? “I have kept for myself seven thousand men who have not bowed the knee to Baal.” So too at the present time there is a remnant (leimma), chosen by grace (kat’ eklogēn charitos).’ The theological structure: the hiddenness of the remnant is not a sign of YHWH’s defeat but of his sovereign preservation of what belongs to him — the same structure that underlies the elect-within-Israel argument of Rom 9-11 and the church’s existence as a hidden remnant in every age of apparent apostasy.
1: wayyaggēd ʾaḥāḇ lᵉʾîzeḇel ʾēṯ kol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh ʾēlîyāhû wᵉʾēṯ kol-ʾăšer hārag ʾeṯ-kol-hanᵉḇiʾîm beḥāreḇ — Ahab told Jezebel everything Elijah had done and how he had killed all the prophets with the sword. The messenger report to Jezebel is ironically the inverse of Obadiah's report to Ahab (18:16): each report triggers the next scene. Ahab's telling is neutral — he reports facts without apparent evaluation, leaving Jezebel to draw her own conclusion and act.
2: wattišlaḥ ʾîzeḇel malʾāḵ ʾel-ʾēlîyāhû lēʾmōr kōh-yaʿăśûn ʾĕlōhîm wᵉḵōh yōsîpû — Jezebel sent a messenger to Elijah saying, 'so may the gods do to me and even more.' This is the standard self-curse oath formula used to guarantee a threatened action. ʾĔlōhîm here is plural (the gods — her Phoenician deities), not the God of Israel. Kî-ʾim-šîṯî ʾeṯ-napšᵉḵā kᵉnepeš ʾaḥaḏ mēhem — if I do not make your life like the life of one of them by this time tomorrow. The threat is specific and timed — she gives Elijah a deadline, apparently to allow him to flee rather than be taken by surprise, an odd mercy that may reflect ancient Near Eastern honor conventions around formal threats.
3: wayyarʾ wayyāqom wayyēleḵ ʾel-napšô — when he saw (this), he arose and fled for his life. The MT reads wayyarʾ (he saw), while several manuscripts read wayyirāʾ (he feared). If wayyarʾ is original, Elijah's flight is a response to information, not primarily emotion; if wayyirāʾ, it is fear-driven flight. Either reading produces the same physical response: Elijah runs. Wayyāḇōʾ ʾel-bᵉʾēr šeḇaʿ ʾăšer lîhûḏāh — he came to Beersheba in Judah (the southernmost city of settlement, beyond Ahab's jurisdiction) and left his servant there (wayyaʿăzōḇ ʾeṯ-naʿărô šām).
5: wayyiškaḇ wayyîšan taḥaṯ rōtem ʾeḥāḏ wᵉhinnēh-zeh malʾāḵ nōgēaʿ bô — he lay down and slept under the broom tree (rōtem, Retama raetam), and behold, an angel was touching him. Hinnēh-zeh malʾāḵ (behold, an angel) — the hinnēh + participle construction marks the apparition as sudden and surprising, as in the theophanies of Gen 18:1–2 and 28:12. The angel's touch (nāgaʿ) is gentle, not violent — a provision touch, not a judgment touch. The broom tree (rōtem) is a desert shrub providing minimal but real shade.
6: wayyabbeṭ wᵉhinnēh mᵉraʾăšōṯāyw ʿūgaṯ rᵉṣāpîm wᵉṣappaḥaṯ māyim — he looked, and there at his head was a cake baked on coals (ʿūgāh rᵉṣāpîm — a flat bread baked on hot stones or embers, the same preparation as the widow's cake in 17:13) and a jug of water. The angelic provision mirrors the ravens (17:6) and the Zarephath widow (17:15–16); YHWH provides food for the fleeing prophet through supernatural agency. Wayyōʾkal wayyišṯ wayyāšāḇ wayyiškaḇ — he ate, drank, returned (lay down again), and slept. The prophet's exhaustion is not remedied in one feeding.
7: wayyāšāḇ malʾaḵ YHWH šēnît wayyiggaʿ bô wayyōʾmar qûm ʾĕḵōl kî-rāḇ mimmᵉḵā haddāreḵ — the angel of YHWH came back a second time, touched him again, and said, 'Arise and eat, for the journey is too great for you.' Kî-rāḇ mimmᵉḵā haddāreḵ — because great (rāḇ) from/beyond you is the journey. The 'journey' implied is to Horeb — not yet named, but the provision is calibrated to a 40-day trip. The second provision reinforces that this is not ordinary food but strengthening for a supernatural journey.
8: wayyāqom wayyōʾkal wayyišṭeh wayyēleḵ bᵉḵōaḥ hāʾăḵîlāh hahîʾ ʾarbaʿîm yôm wᵉʾarbaʿîm laylāh ʿaḏ har hāʾĕlōhîm ḥōrēḇ — he arose, ate, drank, and traveled in the strength of that food forty days and forty nights to Horeb, the mountain of God. The Moses parallel is explicit: Exod 34:28 (and Deut 9:9, 18) records Moses' forty days and forty nights at Horeb/Sinai without food or water. Har hāʾĕlōhîm ḥōrēḇ (the mountain of God, Horeb) identifies the destination as the Sinai theophany site — Elijah's journey is a recapitulation of Moses' ascent to receive divine revelation.
9: wayyāḇōʾ šām ʾel-hamᵉʿārāh wayyālen šām wᵉhinnēh dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾēlāyw wayyōʾmer lô mah-lᵉḵā pōh ʾēlîyāhû — he came to a cave (hamᵉʿārāh, with definite article — possibly the 'the' cave, i.e., a specific known cave at Horeb, perhaps the cleft where Moses stood in Exod 33:22) and lodged there. The divine question mah-lᵉḵā pōh ʾēlîyāhû (what are you doing here, Elijah?) is not a request for information but a rhetorical challenge: the prophet should not be at the mountain of God fleeing; he should be in Israel working. The question will be repeated in v.13 after the theophany, expecting a different response.
10: qannōʾ qinnēʾṯî laYHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣᵉḇāʾôṯ — I have been utterly zealous (qannōʾ qinnēʾṯî, infinitive absolute + perfect for intensification) for YHWH God of hosts. Elijah's lament names three charges against Israel: they abandoned (ʿāzᵃḇû) your covenant, tore down (hārāsû) your altars, killed (hārᵃgû) your prophets with the sword. Then: wāʾiwwāṯēr ʾănî lᵉḇaddî wayyᵉḇaqšû ʾeṯ-napšî lᵉqaḥtāh — I alone am left and they seek my life to take it. The 'I alone' (ʾănî lᵉḇaddî) picks up his claim at Carmel (18:22) but now in the context of despair rather than challenge. Elijah's self-perception is of total prophetic isolation.
11: wayyōʾmer ṣēʾ wᵉʿāmadtā bāhār lipnê YHWH wᵉhinnēh YHWH ʿōḇēr wᵉrûaḥ gᵉḏōlāh wᵉḥāzāq mᵉparēq hārîm ûmᵉšabbēr sᵉlāʿîm lipnê YHWH lōʾ ḇārûaḥ YHWH — go out and stand on the mountain before YHWH. A great and strong wind that splits mountains and shatters rocks — but YHWH was not in the wind. The three negative theophany elements (wind, earthquake, fire) follow the classic Sinai pattern (Exod 19:16–19), yet each is negated: YHWH is not located in the spectacular phenomena. The sequence prepares for the voice of thin silence (v.12): the revelation comes not in power-display but in stillness.
13: wayyihî kišmōaʿ ʾēlîyāhû wayyāleṭ pānāyw bᵉʾaddartô wayyēṣēʾ wayyaʿămōd pēṯaḥ hamᵉʿārāh — when Elijah heard it, he wrapped his face in his mantle (bᵉʾaddartô, H155) and went out and stood at the entrance of the cave. ʾAddereṯ is the prophetic mantle — the same garment transferred to Elisha in v.19. Wrapping the face before the divine presence echoes Moses hiding his face at the burning bush (Exod 3:6). The repeated question: mah-lᵉḵā pōh ʾēlîyāhû — what are you doing here, Elijah? The theophany has not changed Elijah's answer; its function is to reframe the question.
14: wayyōʾmar qannōʾ qinnēʾṯî laYHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣᵉḇāʾôṯ kî-ʿāzᵃḇû ḇᵉrîṯᵉḵā bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl — Elijah repeats the same lament verbatim (cf. v.10). The repetition is a literary signal that the prophet has not processed the theophany — the spectacular phenomena followed by the still small voice have not shifted his self-perception. YHWH does not repeat the theophany; instead he issues three commissions (vv.15–17) and the corrective revelation of the 7,000 (v.18), overriding Elijah's tunnel vision with structural information about the covenant community.
15: wayyōʾmer YHWH ʾēlāyw leḵ šûḇ lᵉḏarkeḵā miḏbārāh ḏammāśeq — YHWH said to him, go, return by your way to the wilderness of Damascus. The commission šûḇ lᵉḏarkeḵā (return by your way) ends the Horeb episode: Elijah must re-engage the political world he fled. Three anointing-commissions follow: Hazael king over Aram (ûmāšaḥtā ʾeṯ-ḥăzāʾēl lᵉmelek ʿal-ʾărām). Māšaḥ (to anoint) is the technical verb for royal installation — Elijah is commissioned to anoint a foreign king, an extraordinary prophetic act.
16: wᵉʾeṯ-yēhûʾ ben-nimšî ṯimšaḥ lᵉmelek ʿal-yiśrāʾēl wᵉʾeṯ-ʾĕlîšāʿ ben-šāpāṭ miʾāḇēl mᵉḥôlāh ṯimšaḥ lᵉnāḇîʾ taḥteḵā — Jehu son of Nimshi you shall anoint as king over Israel, and Elisha son of Shaphat from Abel-meholah you shall anoint as prophet in your place. The three commissions form a historical program: Hazael (Aram), Jehu (Israel), Elisha (prophet-succession) — each will carry out YHWH's judgment on Baal-Israel. Notably, Elijah will execute only the last (v.19); the others are fulfilled through Elisha's ministry (2 Kgs 8:13; 9:1–6), but the commissions stand as YHWH's sovereign plan announced here.
17: wᵉhāyāh hammimmalēṭ mēḥereḇ ḥăzāʾēl yāmîṯ yēhûʾ ûhammimmalēṭ mēḥereḇ yēhûʾ yāmîṯ ʾĕlîšāʿ — whoever escapes Hazael's sword, Jehu will kill; whoever escapes Jehu's sword, Elisha will kill. The triplet of instruments creates a theological net with no escape for the apostate: three successive divine agents cover all contingencies. The use of the prophetic ministry of Elisha as a 'killing' instrument is unusual — this functions as divine judgment through prophetic word and action, not physical violence.
19: wayyēleḵ miššām wayyimṣāʾ ʾeṯ-ʾĕlîšāʿ ben-šāpāṭ wᵉhûʾ ḥōrēš šᵉnêm-ʿāśār ṣᵉmādîm lᵉpānāyw wᵉhûʾ biṯnêm ʿāśār — he went from there and found Elisha son of Shaphat plowing with twelve yoke of oxen before him, he being with the twelfth. The twelve yoke of oxen suggests a large estate — Elisha's family is prosperous. Wayyaʿăḇōr ʾēlîyāhû ʾēlāyw wayyašleḵ ʾaddartô ʾēlāyw — Elijah passed by him and threw his mantle (ʾaddereṯ) onto him. The casting of the prophetic mantle is the commissioning act — no words are recorded, the gesture carries the prophetic call. The mantle (Elijah's in v.13) becomes the symbol of prophetic succession; it will reappear at the Jordan crossing (2 Kgs 2:8, 13–14).
20: wayyaʿăzōḇ ʾeṯ-habbāqār wayyārāṣ ʾaḥărê ʾēlîyāhû wayyōʾmar ʾeššᵉqāh-nāʾ lᵉʾāḇî wᵉlᵉʾimmî wᵉʾēlᵉḵāh ʾaḥărêḵā — he left the oxen and ran after Elijah and said, let me kiss my father and my mother goodbye and I will follow you. Wayyōʾmer lô leḵ šûḇ kî meh ʿāśîṯî lāḵ — Elijah said, go back, for what have I done to you? The reply is enigmatic: some read it as granting the farewell leave ('go back [and say goodbye]'); others as a challenge ('return to what I have done to you' — i.e., take the mantle seriously). Either reading results in Elisha completing the farewell and then following.
21: wayyāšāḇ mēʾaḥărāyw wayyiqqaḥ ʾeṯ-ṣeḇeḏ habbāqār wayyizbaḥēhû ûḇikᵉlê habbāqār biššᵉlām — he turned back, took the yoke of oxen, slaughtered them, and boiled their flesh with the wooden equipment of the oxen (bikᵉlê habbāqār — literally the implements of the oxen, i.e., the wooden yoke and plow). Using the farm equipment as fuel for the boiling fire is an acted commitment: the tools of his former livelihood become the fuel for the commissioning feast, and the oxen become the commissioning sacrifice. There is no going back — the instruments of agricultural life are burned. Wayyittēn lāʿām wayyōʾḵēlû — he gave it to the people and they ate. Then he rose and followed Elijah and served him (wayᵉšārᵉṯēhû).
